title: Kundalini and Parenting: How to Function as a Parent During Spiritual Emergency
meta_description: You're here because you're scared. You're probably exhausted, wired, feeling things you can't name, and you have kids. That's the nightmare, isn't it? The
body:
Kundalini and Parenting: How to Function as a Parent During Spiritual Emergency

You're here because you're scared. You're probably exhausted, wired, feeling things you can't name, and you have kids. That's the nightmare, isn't it? The world is falling apart inside your head and body, and these little humans depend on you to be… normal. Stable. Present. And you're anything but. I get it. I've been there. That feeling of being utterly alone, like you're losing your mind, while simultaneously trying to make sure your kid has clean socks and gets to school on time? It's a special kind of hell. But you're not alone, and you're not crazy. You're in a spiritual emergency, and you're a parent. That's a brutal combination, but you can get through it. You really can.

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. It's going to be hard. Probably the hardest thing you'll ever do. But you're stronger than you think. You have to be. For them, and for you. We're going to talk about practical survival - not just for you, but for your family. This isn't about enlightenment; it's about not breaking, about keeping your head above water when the energetic ocean inside you is trying to drown you. Right?

The Absolute Basics: Survival Mode for You and Them

First off, let's just admit it: you're not operating at 100%. You're probably at 10%, maybe 20% on a good day. So, you need to strip everything back to the bare bones. What absolutely, non-negotiably needs to happen for your kids to be fed, safe, and somewhat looked after? That's your new baseline. Everything else is a bonus. Drop the guilt about what you should be doing. You're in a crisis. This isn't normal life, and you can't expect normal output from yourself.

Here's the thing: you're not going to be able to meditate your way out of this while simultaneously making organic kale smoothies and leading a Montessori-inspired play session. Forget it. Seriously. Let go of all those expectations. You're in survival mode. Your kids are in survival mode with you, whether they know it or not. So, what does that look like?

Prioritize Sleep (Yours, Theirs, Anyone's)

Sleep might feel impossible. Your body is buzzing, your mind is racing, you're having visions or intense sensations. I know. I've spent nights staring at the ceiling, feeling like I was being electrocuted from the inside out. But sleep, even broken, restless sleep, is your most powerful medicine right now. And for your kids? Consistent sleep for them is non-negotiable. Their routine, their stability, depends on it. If they're sleeping, you get a chance to rest, even if it's just lying down in the dark.

  • For you: Don't fight it. If you can only get 30 minutes, take it. If you feel a tiny window of calm, lie down. Don't worry about "deep" sleep. Any rest is good rest. Cut out caffeine, sugar - anything that will make the internal buzzing worse. You know what I mean? You're already overstimulated; you don't need more.
  • For them: Stick to their bedtime routines like glue. Even if you're a zombie, go through the motions. Bath, story, bed. The predictability is a comfort for them and a structure for you. If they're old enough, explain, "Mommy/Daddy isn't feeling well, so we need to make sure you get extra rest." Don't scare them, but be honest within their understanding.

Food and Basic Needs: Keep it Simple

Cooking might feel like climbing Everest. Grocery shopping might feel like navigating a minefield. So, simplify. Massively simplify. What can you quickly throw together? What can someone else bring? Don't be too proud to accept help, or even ask for it.

  • For you: Easy, bland, nourishing food. Toast, soup, rice, plain chicken. Things that won't upset your already sensitive system. Don't starve yourself, but don't force elaborate meals. Hydrate. Drink water. Lots of it. You're burning through a lot of energy.
  • For them: Whatever they'll eat that keeps them fed. If it's pasta every night for a week, so be it. If it's frozen chicken nuggets, fine. This isn't the time for gourmet meals or perfectly balanced nutrition. It's about survival. You can get back to being a food blogger later. Right now, it's about sustenance.

And hygiene? Showers for everyone. Clean clothes, mostly. That's about it. Don't worry about a spotless house. A clean enough house is fine. Nobody's judging you, and if they are, they don't understand what you're going through anyway.

Finding Support: You Can't Do This Alone

Look, you can't. You just can't. This isn't a solo mission. This crisis is bigger than you, and you've got little ones depending on you. You need help. And I know how hard it is to ask, especially when you feel like you're losing your mind and don't want anyone to see it. But here's the truth: people who love you will want to help. And those who don't understand? They don't need to understand everything, just that you're going through a rough patch.

Who Can You Trust?

This is where it gets tricky. You need people who are stable, who aren't going to freak out, and who can handle you being less than your usual self. Maybe it's a partner, a parent, a close friend, a sibling. Someone who can step in for a few hours, take the kids to the park, make them dinner, or just be present so you can go lie down in a dark room. You know who your people are. The ones who don't judge, who just show up.

  • Be honest (within limits): You don't have to tell them you're having a full-blown kundalini awakening with visions and energetic surges. You can say you're dealing with a severe, inexplicable health crisis, extreme exhaustion, or an intense spiritual experience that's overwhelming. "I'm really unwell right now, and I need help with the kids." That's enough for most people.
  • Delegate: Don't try to manage the help. If someone offers to bring food, say yes. If they offer to take the kids for an afternoon, say yes. If they offer to do laundry, say yes. Give them specific tasks. "Can you take the kids to the playground for an hour?" "Can you pick up groceries: milk, bread, bananas?" Make it easy for them to help you.

Professional Help: Don't Be Afraid

This is crucial. You need someone who understands spiritual emergencies. Not just any therapist. Not someone who will immediately label you psychotic and medicate you into oblivion. You need someone who can hold space for what you're experiencing, who understands that these are real phenomena, not just symptoms of mental illness. I've seen too many people misdiagnosed and mistreated because their experiences didn't fit neatly into Western psychiatric boxes.

  • Research: Look for transpersonal therapists, spiritual emergence coaches, or therapists who specialize in spiritual crises. Read books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) by Stanislav and Christina Grof. It's a lifesaver. Another one that helped me make sense of things is The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). These resources can give you language for what you're feeling and help you find practitioners who get it.
  • Be discerning: If a therapist immediately jumps to medication without listening to your experience, they might not be the right fit. You're not refusing medication out of hand - sometimes it's necessary to stabilize. But you need someone who sees the bigger picture, who respects your spiritual journey, even if it's a terrifying one. Someone who helps you contain and integrate, not just suppress.

Managing Your Internal World While Present for Them

This is the tightrope walk. You're having these insane, intense experiences - energy surges, visions, emotional rollercoasters, existential dread - and then your kid asks for a juice box. How do you pivot? How do you stay grounded enough to respond appropriately?

Grounding Techniques: Your Lifelines

You need quick, on-the-fly grounding techniques. You won't always have time to sit for 20 minutes. Sometimes it's 30 seconds in the bathroom, or while you're stirring dinner. Honestly, anything that brings you back to your body, to the present moment, is gold.

  • Breath: This is your number one tool. Deep breaths. Slow exhales. Even just three conscious breaths can shift something. Do it when you feel overwhelmed. Do it before you respond to a child's tantrum. Do it when you feel like you're floating away.
  • Senses: Focus on what you can see, hear, touch, smell, taste. The texture of your clothes, the sound of your child's voice, the smell of dinner cooking (or burning, let's be real). The cold water on your hands. This pulls you out of the internal chaos and into the external reality.
  • Movement: Gentle movement. A short walk, stretching, even just shaking out your hands and feet. Physical activity helps discharge some of that intense energy. If you can't leave the house, just pace in the living room, or do some simple stretches while your kids are playing.

Explaining Without Scaring

Your kids will notice. They're perceptive. You're probably more withdrawn, more irritable, sometimes spacey. You can't pretend everything is fine, because it's not, and they'll pick up on the incongruence. But you also can't dump your spiritual emergency on them. So, what do you say?

  • Simple language: "Mommy/Daddy is feeling a bit under the weather right now." "My body is feeling very sensitive." "I need some quiet time because my head feels buzzy."
  • Reassurance: Always follow up with reassurance. "But I love you very much, and I'm here for you." "This isn't your fault." "I'm working on feeling better."
  • Boundaries: It's okay to say, "Mommy needs five minutes of quiet, then I'll play with you." Or, "I can't lift you right now because my body feels funny." Teach them that it's okay for people to have needs, and you're modeling self-care, even if it's extreme self-care.

You'll find more insights into this dance between internal experience and external demands in books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). It really helps to know you're not the only one trying to figure out how to live a normal life while your inner world is exploding. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) offers practical guidance for managing the physical and psychological aspects.

Acceptance and Patience: The Long Haul

This isn't a sprint. It's a marathon, and sometimes it feels like an ultra-marathon through a desert while you're on fire. You need to cultivate radical acceptance of your situation. This is happening. It's not going away overnight. And you have kids. So, you have to find a way to be in it, with them.

Let Go of Control

You probably had a vision for how you'd parent. Forget it. Throw it out the window. You're not that parent right now. You're the parent who is surviving a spiritual emergency. And that's okay. Control is an illusion anyway, especially when your nervous system is going haywire. Let go of the need to control every aspect of your kids' lives, your house, your schedule. Just let things be messy. Does that land? Because it's hard, I know. It's like letting go of a part of yourself.

Be Patient with Yourself (and Them)

You're going to have bad days. Days where you snap, days where you cry, days where you just want to hide under the covers. That's okay. You're human. You're under immense pressure. Apologize to your kids if you mess up. "I'm sorry I yelled, sweetie. Mommy is having a hard time today." They'll learn empathy, and they'll learn that even adults make mistakes. They'll learn forgiveness. Truth is, you're teaching them a lot about resilience just by getting through this.

And be patient with the process itself. Kundalini awakenings can take years to integrate. There will be ups and downs, periods of calm, and then intense resurfacings. Don't expect a quick fix. Just keep putting one foot in front of the other. Keep showing up. Keep breathing.

The Long-Term View: Integration and Growth

Eventually, this will stabilize. It will. It might feel like it never will, but it does. And when it does, you'll be a different person. A much stronger, more aware, more compassionate person. You'll have walked through fire. And you'll have done it while being a parent. That's a superpower, honestly.

When the intensity starts to wane, you'll begin the process of integration. This is where you make sense of what happened, how it changed you, and how to live in your new normal. You'll find new ways of being with your children, new depths of connection. You'll have a deep understanding of suffering and resilience that most people never touch. And that, in itself, is a gift you'll pass on to your children, not through words, but through your very being.

So yeah, it's terrifying. It's overwhelming. But you're not broken. You're breaking open. And you're doing it with tiny hands holding onto yours. You've got this. One breath, one meal, one moment at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
